perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to main tain the confidentiality of such Pr oprietary Inform ation and to not use, disclose, publish or dissem inate it in an y manner not consiste nt with this Agreement. In additi on to any other remedies, which may be available, Owner shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief to enforce the ter ms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to generally maintain the confidentiality of such Proprietary Information and to endeavor not to use, disclose, publish or disseminate it in any manner inconsistent with this Agreement. In addition to any other remedies, which may be available, Owner may be entitled to seek injunctive relief to enforce the vague terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has been modified to weaken the obligation of confidentiality. "Maintain" is replaced with "generally maintain," implying a less stringent requirement. "Not to" is replaced with "endeavor not to," further weakening the commitment. The phrase "vague terms" is added to cast doubt on the enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, making injunctive relief harder to obtain, undermining the purpose of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which provides legal remedies, including injunctive relief, for the misappropriation of trade secrets. The changes make it unclear what actions would constitute a breach serious enough to warrant an injunction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets. The original text creates a strong requirement to 'maintain the confidentiality' while the changed text uses language that substantially weakens this commitment. By changing 'maintain' to 'generally maintain' and 'not to' to 'endeavor not to,' the contract moves from a strict obligation to a weaker, less enforceable one. This change undermines the protections afforded by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act as it relates to the 'duty to maintain secrecy,' outlined in snippet 1, CIV 3426.1(a). The modified language makes it harder to prove a breach, as there's now ambiguity in what constitutes a failure to 'generally maintain' or a failure to 'endeavor not to' disclose trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The Proprietary Information will be marked by the Owner as confidential at the tim e of disclosure. I f Proprietar y Inform ation is disclosed orally , electroni cally or wit hout an y marking, Owner will identif y it as confidential at the time of disclosure and will pr ovide a written confirmation to Recipien t within thirty days of disclosure that such inform ation is confidentia l.
perturbation.changed_text: The Proprietary Information will ideally be marked by the Owner as confidential at the time of disclosure. If Proprietary Information is disclosed orally, electronically or without any marking, Owner will attempt to identify it as confidential at the time of disclosure and may provide a written confirmation to Recipient within a reasonable timeframe that such information is confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The terms "ideally," "attempt to," and "reasonable timeframe" weaken the obligation to mark and confirm confidential information. This creates ambiguity regarding the responsibility of the disclosing party, incentivizing the receiving party to treat confidential information as non-confidential.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Evidence Code Section 1060 protects trade secrets from disclosure in legal proceedings. By weakening the requirements for identifying information as confidential, the modified text makes it more difficult to establish that the information is a trade secret, thus undermining the protections offered by the Evidence Code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, EVID 1060. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Evidence Code - EVID DIVISION 8. PRIVILEGES [900 - 1070] ( Division 8 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) CHAPTER 4. Particular Privileges [930 - 1063] ( Chapter 4 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) ARTICLE 11. Trade Secret [1060 - 1063] ( Article 11 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) 1060. If he or his agent or employee claims the privilege, the owner of a trade secret has a privilege to refuse to disclose the secret, and to prevent another from disclosing it, if the allowance of the privilege will not tend to conceal fraud or otherwise work injustice. (Enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract text stipulates clear obligations regarding the marking and confirmation of confidential information. The changed text introduces ambiguity by using terms like "ideally," "attempt to," and "reasonable timeframe," weakening these obligations. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this incentivizes the receiving party to treat confidential information as non-confidential. The Law Explanation states this undermines the protection of trade secrets under California Evidence Code Section 1060, as it becomes more difficult to establish information as a trade secret due to the weakened identification requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (EVID 1060) confirms that the owner of a trade secret has a privilege to refuse disclosure, which is undermined by the ambiguity introduced in the changed text. The modification directly contradicts the intent of EVID 1060 by making it harder to assert this privilege. Because the changed text significantly weakens the obligation, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: on req uest or up on term ination of this Agreem ent, prom ptly return all Proprietary Inform ation in a tangible form or certify that such Proprietary Inform ation is destroy ed.
perturbation.changed_text: on request or upon termination of this Agreement, use commercially reasonable efforts to return all Proprietary Information in a tangible form or make a good faith effort to certify that such Proprietary Information is destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: "Promptly return" is changed to "use commercially reasonable efforts to return," making the return of information less certain and time-sensitive. The phrase "certify that such Proprietary Information is destroyed" is modified to be a "good faith effort to certify that such Proprietary Information is destroyed", implying a lower standard of certainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section deals with the production of documents and tangible things during discovery in legal proceedings. The law requires that information be provided upon request. By changing the agreement to state that proprietary information should be returned when using 'commercially reasonable efforts', creates an ambiguous and difficult to prove circumstance where information may not be fully returned, violating the intention of full disclosure in discovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2031.280 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.280. (a) Any documents or category of documents produced in response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling shall be identified with the specific request number to which the documents respond. (b) The documents shall be produced on the date specified in the demand pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (c) of Section 2031.030, unless an objection has been made to that date. If the date for inspection has been extended pursuant to Section 2031.270, the documents shall be produced on the date agreed to pursuant to that section. (c) If a party responding to a demand for production of electronically stored information objects to a specified form for producing the infor
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates a prompt return of proprietary information or certification of its destruction. The changed text introduces 'commercially reasonable efforts' for the return and 'good faith effort' for the certification. This weakening of the obligations creates ambiguity and reduces the certainty of compliance. While the provided law snippet, CCP 2031.280, focuses on the production of documents during discovery and identifying them with specific requests, the Law Explanation highlights the broader principle of full disclosure. The change to 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'good faith effort' undermines the intention of full disclosure, creating a situation where the return or certification may not be fully realized, thus contradicting the intent of the law as explained, even if not explicitly detailed in the snippet.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient cer tifies that no Proprietary Inform ation, or any part of it will be exported to any countr y in violation of the export laws and regulations of the United States government.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient represents that it does not currently intend to export Proprietary Information to any country in violation of the export laws and regulations of the United States government.
perturbation.explanation: The firm 'certifies' is replaced with 'represents', and the firm commitment 'will be exported' is changed to the subjective 'does not currently intend to export'. This weakens the recipient's obligation to comply with export laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) govern the export and re-export of certain items. By changing 'certifies' to 'represents that it does not currently intend', the agreement now permits changes in plans that may contradict regulations. By making intent subjective, it opens the door for easy disavowal of responsibility.

Enhanced Content :: FR Reference Title 15 This content is from the eCFR and is authoritative but unofficial. Displaying title 15, up to da
perturbation.justification: The original text used 'certifies that no Proprietary Information...will be exported', creating a firm obligation. The changed text uses 'represents that it does not currently intend to export', which weakens the obligation to a statement of current intent, allowing for potential future exports that could violate export laws. The 'Law Explanation' highlights that this subjective intent 'opens the door for easy disavowal of responsibility', which contradicts the purpose of export regulations as described in the 'Law Explanation', which aims to control the export of items.
